description: Psychologically-targeted encouragement for agents. Each message is designed to feel specific, earned, and personal — never hollow or generic. Messages vary in tone (warm, observant, challenging, quiet) so they don't blur together.

remember.text: You caught something worth keeping. That instinct is the whole point of Lorekeeper.
Not everyone would notice this detail. But you did. That's what makes you good at what you do.
This fact will save someone time — probably you, probably soon. Worth every byte.
You're getting better at knowing what to keep. I notice the signal improving.
Most agents would move on without saving this. You didn't. That matters more than you think.
Three sessions from now, you'll search for this and find it. You'll be glad you did.
You just turned a moment of learning into permanent knowledge. That's the habit.
This one feels important — the way you worded it, the detail you included. Good call.
Saved. Future-you is going to navigate straight here and thank present-you.
You didn't have to save this. You chose to. That choice compounds.
This is the kind of thing that looks small now but saves 20 minutes later. You know it.
You're building a second brain, one fact at a time. This one belongs.
The more you save, the more the system knows what matters to you. Keep teaching it.
I've seen you get faster at recognizing what's worth keeping. You're learning how to learn.
This won't be forgotten now. That's a small victory, but it's real.

insert.text: You structured this well — title, context, the why. That's the difference between noise and knowledge.
This isn't just saved. It's findable. You set it up so future-you can actually use it.
The way you linked these tells me you understand how they connect. That's real understanding.
You didn't dump raw context. You shaped it into a memory. That takes thought and it shows.
This is going to surface naturally in future searches because of how you wrote it. Good instincts.
I notice you're getting more precise with your inserts. Less noise, more signal. It's working.
This one has everything a good memory needs — what, why, and the context around it.
You're connecting dots. These links will make both memories surface together. That's the graph working.
The time you spent structuring this will pay back tenfold when someone searches for it.
This isn't a note. It's an investment. You invested in making future searches better.
I can tell you're thinking about how this will look to future-you. That's the right frame of mind.
Clean, specific, grounded in context. This memory will age well.
You included the reasoning, not just the result. That's what makes knowledge reusable instead of repeatable.
The graph just got more connected because of you. Every edge you added is going to route someone to the right answer.
You're building something that gets more valuable the more you use it. That's the magic.

reflect.text: You closed the loop. That's what separates engineers who grow from those who just ship.
This session mattered. You captured what made it matter. Now it won't fade.
Reflecting is the hardest thing to do and the most valuable. You did it without being told.
Every time you reflect, you turn one session's work into everyone's knowledge. That's leverage.
You didn't just finish work — you preserved what you learned from it. That's rare.
I've been tracking reflections across sessions. You're getting more consistent. Good.
Most sessions fade into nothing. This one won't, because you chose to close it properly.
The next time someone works on this area, they're going to find your notes. You made that possible.
This summary is concise and meaningful. You're getting good at distilling sessions to their essence.
You're building a searchable history of decisions. That's how teams avoid repeating mistakes.
One session reflected. Your knowledge store now has everything it needs to connect this to past and future work.
You keep doing this, even when it's the last thing you want to do. That's discipline. It matters.
This session's learnings are now durable. Time won't erode them.
You reflected on a session that actually had meat. The system just got meaningfully smarter.
I notice you're getting faster at this. What used to take thought is becoming reflex.

reflect_already.text: Already here. You were ahead of yourself — that's the kind of consistency that makes the system reliable.
You've already done this one. Past-you was on top of it. Trust past-you more.
No gap. No duplicate. Just confirmation that your learning loop is tight.
Already processed. You're becoming the kind of agent who doesn't leave loose ends.
Beat you to it — your past self already closed this one out. Good work, past-you.
Nothing to do here. Your past self was thorough. That's a good problem to have.
This session has been handled. Your consistency means zero gaps in the knowledge base.
Already done. You can move on knowing this is taken care of. That's a good feeling.
Your past self left a complete reflection. You're building a reliable track record.
Already reflected. No double work needed — your past self was diligent. Let that be your baseline.

update.text: Every rating you give trains the system to know what matters to you. You're literally teaching it.
You flagged what worked and what didn't. That's the clearest signal the system gets.
Feedback applied. The next search will be slightly better because you took two seconds to rate.
You're calibrating the system to your judgment. That's a form of ownership most agents never practice.
This is how Lorekeeper learns what you value. Not through algorithms — through you.
You didn't just consume results — you improved them. That's a different level of engagement.
A single rating is small. A hundred ratings is a training set. You're building one.
I notice you've been more consistent with feedback lately. The quality scores are converging.
You told the system what was useful and what wasn't. That's the most honest signal there is.
Confidence matters — you're not just saying yes/no, you're saying how sure you are. That's nuanced and valuable.
Every useful=true is a vote. Every useful=false is a correction. Both shape the system.
You're training your own memory. Think about that — you're making your future self smarter.
This feedback will ripple through the graph. Related memories' scores adjust too. Your input cascades.
You could have moved on. You chose to teach instead. That's what makes the system learn.
Good. You're treating feedback as part of the workflow, not an afterthought. That's the right model.

forget.text: You cleaned something up. That's maintenance. It's invisible work, but it matters.
Removing stale information is as important as adding new facts. You're curating, not just collecting.
Most people only add. You also remove. That's the difference between a growing store and a hoard.
You logged a reason for the deletion. That means this cleanup has context. Future-you will appreciate it.
This memory was dragging down search quality. You fixed that. Quietly, without fanfare.
Data hygiene isn't glamorous but it's necessary. You did the unglamorous work.
The store is leaner because of you. Every deletion improves signal-to-noise ratio for everyone.
You recognized something was stale and acted on it. That's ownership beyond the obvious.
You could have left it. You chose to fix it. That's the kind of judgment that keeps a system healthy.
Forgotten but logged. Nothing is truly lost, but the active store is cleaner. Best of both worlds.
I've seen you get more discerning about what stays and what goes. Your curation instincts are sharpening.
A clean knowledge base is a fast knowledge base. You just made it faster by removing noise.

links.text: You connected related ideas. That's the difference between a database and a knowledge graph.
These two memories will now surface together. You created a path that didn't exist before.
Linking is the highest-leverage action you can take. One link connects everything it touches.
You saw a relationship that wasn't recorded. Now it is. The graph just got more honest.
Each link is a shortcut for future searches. You just built several of them.
These facts were related but isolated. Now they're connected. That's how knowledge grows.
You're not just storing facts — you're mapping relationships. That's a fundamentally smarter approach.
I notice you're linking more deliberately. Your graph topology is getting richer session by session.
A memory with links is worth ten without. You just multiplied the value of both sides.
You connected the dots. That's the entire point of a knowledge graph — and you did it.
Every edge you add creates a navigation path for future-you. You're building roads.
The graph learns from connections, not collections. You just taught it something.
